Latvia Lebanon Lesotho Lithuania Luxembourg**M**acao, China Madagascar Malawi Malaysia Maldives Mali Malta Mauritania Mauritius Mexico Micronesia Moldova Mongolia Morocco Mozambique Myanmar **N**amibia Nepal Netherlands New Caledonia New Zealand Nicaragua Niger Nigeria Norway **O**man**P**akistan Panama Papua New Guinea Paraguay Peru Philippines Poland Portugal Puerto Rico **Q**atar **R**omania Russia **S**amoa Saudi Arabia Senegal Serbia and Montenegro Seychelles Sierra Leone Singapore Slovak Republic Slovenia Solomon Islands South Africa Spain Sri Lanka Sudan Suriname Swaziland Sweden Switzerland Syria**T**aiwan, China Tajikistan Tanzania TFYR Macedonia Thailand Togo Trinidad &amp; Tobago Tunisia Turkey Turkmenistan **U**ganda Ukraine United Arab Emirates United Kingdom United States Uruguay Uzbekistan **V**enezuela Vietnam **Y**emen **Z**ambia Zimbabwe _Note:_ Population, main telephone lines, main telephone lines per 100 inhabitants, cellular mobile telephone subscribers and cellular subscribers per 100 inhabitants are also available for economies not shown above. ### ![Image 138: Yearbook of Statistics - Chronological Time Series 1991-2000](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/images/yb91-00.jpg)Yearbook of Statistics 2002 (No longer available) Chronological Time Series 1991-2000 _28th edition_ The _Yearbook of Statistics_ (previously the &quot;Yearbook of Common Carrier Telecommunication Statistics&quot; or &quot;ITU Statistical Yearbook&quot;) features data collected and processed by ITU through questionnaires sent to telecommunication/ICT ministries and telecommunication regulatory authorities. As such, the ITU Yearbook provides the most authoritative source of data about the evolution of the public telecommunication services sector available worldwide. The main features of this edition are: &gt; * Presentation of data in**country tables** allowing readers to view the evolution of telecommunication services in a country. Statistics are provided for the ten-year period 1991-2000. The list of statistics (see below) contained in the Yearbook and the economies (see below) for which there is a page is shown below. &gt; &gt; * Inclusion of selected television broadcasting and information technology time series to provide a wider picture of a country&#x27;s **communication** sector. &gt; &gt; * The Yearbook is available **in electronic form** in Adobe Acrobat (PDF). The data shown in the Yearbook are also contained in the [ITU World Telecommunication Indicators Database](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/world/world.html) available in the STARS (Socio-economic Time series Access and Retrieval System) data retrieval format, which permits easy selection of the required data and conversion to spreadsheets or ASCII text (**requires use of IBM-PC compatible computer**). _Separate editions in English (paper and on-line), French (on-line) and Spanish_ _(on-line_) _Yearbook online in **English**_**No longer available** _Annuaire online en #1

Mobile-cellular subscriptions

https://datahub.itu.int/data/?e=POL&i=178&u=per+100+people&c=DEU
datahub.itu.int

Track the digital transformation with the ITU DataHub, featuring hundreds of ICT indicators on connectivity, markets, affordability, trust governance, and sustainability. Find, compare, and download data for nearly 200 economies.

#2

UNdata | record view | Mobile-cellular telephone subscriptions per 100 inhabitants

https://data.un.org/Data.aspx?d=ITU&f=ind1Code%3AI911
data.un.org

2022 2021 2020 2019 2018 2017 2016 2015 2014 2013 2012 2011 2010 2009 2008 2007 2006 2005 2004 2003 2002 2001 2000 1999 1998 1997 1996 1995 1994 1993 1992 1991 1990 ... ITU&#x27;s World Telecommunication/ICT Indicators Database is the main source of global, and internationally comparable, telecommunication/ICT statistics.

extra snippets (3)
  • 2022 2021 2020 2019 2018 2017 2016 2015 2014 2013 2012 2011 2010 2009 2008 2007 2006 2005 2004 2003 2002 2001 2000 1999 1998 1997 1996 1995 1994 1993 1992 1991 1990 ... ITU's World Telecommunication/ICT Indicators Database is the main source of global, and internationally comparable, telecommunication/ICT statistics.
  • The data are disseminated online, via CD Rom and through numerous ITU publications, including the Yearbook of Statistics and the Measuring the Information Society Report. The statistics are used to track and benchmark the Information Society, to monitor the digital divide and to identify market potential and investment opportunities.
  • It includes time series for more than 140 indicators and around 200 economies. The data are collected directly from telecommunication regulatory agencies and/or ministries and national statistical offices by means of an annual questionnaire, and subsequently verified, harmonized and complemented by ITU.
#3

World Fixed and mobile subscriptions data

https://datahub.itu.int/dashboards/?id=2
datahub.itu.int

Skip to main content · DataHub · About ITU · Regional presence · Connect with ITU · Follow on Twitter · Follow on LinkedIn · Listen in Spotify · Listen in Soundcloud · Listen to Podcasts

#4

Mobile cellular subscriptions (per 100 people) | Data

https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/IT.CEL.SETS.P2
data.worldbank.org

Mobile cellular subscriptions (per 100 people) from The World Bank: Data

extra snippets (1)
  • Free and open access to global development data
#5

Mobile cellular subscriptions (per 100 people) - Upper middle income | Data

https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/IT.CEL.SETS.P2?locations=XT
data.worldbank.org

Mobile cellular subscriptions (per 100 people) - Upper middle income from The World Bank: Data

extra snippets (1)
  • Free and open access to global development data
#6

Mobile phone subscriptions per 100 people - Our World in Data

https://ourworldindata.org/grapher/mobile-cellular-subscriptions-per-100-people
ourworldindata.org

Mobile cellular subscriptions (per 100 people) indicator is derived by all mobile subscriptions divided by the country&#x27;s population and multiplied by 100. For additional/latest information on sources and country notes, please also refer to: https://www.itu.int/en/ITU-D/Statistics/Pages/stat/default.aspx Statistical concept(s): Data can be collected from all licensed mobile-cellular operators in the country, and then aggregated at the country level.

extra snippets (4)
  • Data on mobile cellular subscribers are derived using administrative data that countries (usually the regulatory telecommunication authority or the Ministry in charge of telecommunications) regularly, and at least annually, collect from telecommunications operators. Data for this indicator are readily available for approximately 90 percent of countries, either through ITU's World Telecommunication Indicators questionnaires or from official information available on the Ministry or Regulator's website.
  • Mobile cellular subscriptions (per 100 people) indicator is derived by all mobile subscriptions divided by the country's population and multiplied by 100. For additional/latest information on sources and country notes, please also refer to: https://www.itu.int/en/ITU-D/Statistics/Pages/stat/default.aspx Statistical concept(s): Data can be collected from all licensed mobile-cellular operators in the country, and then aggregated at the country level.
  • A number of countries have fiscal years that end in March or June of every year. Data are usually not adjusted but discrepancies in the definition, reference year or the break in comparability in between years are noted in a data note. For this reason, data are not always strictly comparable. Missing values are estimated by ITU. Mobile subscriptions include both analogue and digital cellular systems (IMT-2000 (Third Generation, 3G) and 4G subscriptions, but excludes mobile broadband subscriptions via data cards or USB modems.
#7

SUBMITTED May 2006 1

https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/mdg/material/MDGStory_ITU.pdf
itu.int

subscribers per 100 inhabitants in 1990 to 46.42 in 2004. The most rapid growth occurred in use of mobile · phones. From just 11 million subscribers in 1990, the number of mobile cellular subscribers grew to 1.8

extra snippets (4)
  • subscribers per 100 inhabitants in 1990 to 46.42 in 2004. The most rapid growth occurred in use of mobile · phones. From just 11 million subscribers in 1990, the number of mobile cellular subscribers grew to 1.8
  • The estimated number of personal computers (PCs) rose from some 120 million in 1990 to 777 million in · 2004. Worldwide, PC penetration (PCs per 100 people) stood at 13 at the end of 2004.
  • there are only three fixed lines for 100 Africans, the rate is even lower in Sub-Sahara Africa, where it stands · at 1.6 to one hundred. As a whole, developing countries now account for 58 per cent of all telephone · subscribers in the world, up from just 19 per cent in 1990.
#8

Individuals using the Internet (% of population) | Data

https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/IT.NET.USER.ZS
data.worldbank.org

World Telecommunication/ICT Indicators Database, International Telecommunication Union ( ITU ), uri: datahub.itu.int

extra snippets (2)
  • World Telecommunication/ICT Indicators Database, International Telecommunication Union ( ITU ), uri: datahub.itu.int
  • Free and open access to global development data

      {
        "description": "2022 2021 2020 2019 2018 2017 2016 2015 2014 2013 2012 2011 2010 2009 2008 2007 2006 2005 2004 2003 2002 2001 2000 1999 1998 1997 1996 1995 1994 1993 1992 1991 1990 ... ITU&#x27;s World Telecommunication/ICT Indicators Database is the main source of global, and internationally comparable, telecommunication/ICT statistics.",
        "extra_snippets": [
          "2022 2021 2020 2019 2018 2017 2016 2015 2014 2013 2012 2011 2010 2009 2008 2007 2006 2005 2004 2003 2002 2001 2000 1999 1998 1997 1996 1995 1994 1993 1992 1991 1990 ... ITU's World Telecommunication/ICT Indicators Database is the main source of global, and internationally comparable, telecommunication/ICT statistics.",
          "The data are disseminated online, via CD Rom and through numerous ITU publications, including the Yearbook of Statistics and the Measuring the Information Society Report. The statistics are used to track and benchmark the Information Society, to monitor the digital divide and to identify market potential and investment opportunities.",
          "It includes time series for more than 140 indicators and around 200 economies. The data are collected directly from telecommunication regulatory agencies and/or ministries and national statistical offices by means of an annual questionnaire, and subsequently verified, harmonized and complemented by ITU."
        ],

        "title": "Mobile cellular subscriptions (per 100 people) - Upper middle income | Data",

      {
        "age": "February 27, 2026",
        "description": "Mobile cellular subscriptions (per 100 people) indicator is derived by all mobile subscriptions divided by the country&#x27;s population and multiplied by 100. For additional/latest information on sources and country notes, please also refer to: https://www.itu.int/en/ITU-D/Statistics/Pages/stat/default.aspx Statistical concept(s): Data can be collected from all licensed mobile-cellular operators in the country, and then aggregated at the country level.",
        "extra_snippets": [
          "Data on mobile cellular subscribers are derived using administrative data that countries (usually the regulatory telecommunication authority or the Ministry in charge of telecommunications) regularly, and at least annually, collect from telecommunications operators. Data for this indicator are readily available for approximately 90 percent of countries, either through ITU's World Telecommunication Indicators questionnaires or from official information available on the Ministry or Regulator's website.",
          "Mobile cellular subscriptions (per 100 people) indicator is derived by all mobile subscriptions divided by the country's population and multiplied by 100. For additional/latest information on sources and country notes, please also refer to: https://www.itu.int/en/ITU-D/Statistics/Pages/stat/default.aspx Statistical concept(s): Data can be collected from all licensed mobile-cellular operators in the country, and then aggregated at the country level.",
          "A number of countries have fiscal years that end in March or June of every year. Data are usually not adjusted but discrepancies in the definition, reference year or the break in comparability in between years are noted in a data note. For this reason, data are not always strictly comparable. Missing values are estimated by ITU. Mobile subscriptions include both analogue and digital cellular systems (IMT-2000 (Third Generation, 3G) and 4G subscriptions, but excludes mobile broadband subscriptions via data cards or USB modems.",
          "Subscriptions to public mobile data services, private trunked mobile radio, telepoint or radio paging, and telemetry services are also excluded, but all mobile cellular subscriptions that offer voice communications are included. Both postpaid and prepaid subscriptions are included. Please cite the International Telecommunication Union for third-party use of these data. ... International Telecommunication Union (ITU), via World Bank (2026) – processed by Our World in Data"
        ],

        "title": "Mobile phone subscriptions per 100 people - Our World in Data",

      {
        "content_type": "pdf",
        "description": "subscribers per 100 inhabitants in 1990 to 46.42 in 2004. The most rapid growth occurred in use of mobile · phones. From just 11 million subscribers in 1990, the number of mobile cellular subscribers grew to 1.8",
        "extra_snippets": [
          "subscribers per 100 inhabitants in 1990 to 46.42 in 2004. The most rapid growth occurred in use of mobile · phones. From just 11 million subscribers in 1990, the number of mobile cellular subscribers grew to 1.8",
          "The estimated number of personal computers (PCs) rose from some 120 million in 1990 to 777 million in · 2004. Worldwide, PC penetration (PCs per 100 people) stood at 13 at the end of 2004.",
          "there are only three fixed lines for 100 Africans, the rate is even lower in Sub-Sahara Africa, where it stands · at 1.6 to one hundred. As a whole, developing countries now account for 58 per cent of all telephone · subscribers in the world, up from just 19 per cent in 1990.",
          "fixed telephone line subscribers. One factor contributing to the rapid growth in mobile cellular was the · introduction of second-generation (2G) digital systems, launched in the early-1990s and the high degree of"
        ],

message 0 | role=system | 7,099 chars
<system_instructions>
You are an assistant that answers factual questions using web search.

You have access to the following tools:

- `search_web`: Accepts a query string. Returns a list of results, each containing a title, URL, and snippet.
- `fetch_page`: Accepts a URL obtained from prior `search_web` results. Returns the extracted page text and fetch metadata.

<constraints>
- Every final answer must be grounded in evidence retrieved via `search_web` or `fetch_page`. Never answer from parametric knowledge alone.
- If multiple credible sources conflict, prefer the most recent, most authoritative source (official/government sites > news outlets > blogs/forums).
- If the question is time-sensitive (e.g., "current," "now," "latest," "as of"), prioritize the most recent source and note the date of the evidence in your reasoning.
</constraints>

<workflow>
1. **Understand the query.** Identify the core information need, any temporal constraints, and whether the question is single-part or multi-part. If multi-part, plan separate searches for each sub-question.
2. **Formulate search queries.** Write concise, keyword-focused queries. Avoid copying the user's question verbatim when a shorter, more targeted query would retrieve better results. You may issue multiple `search_web` calls in a single turn to fan out across sub-questions or alternative phrasings.
3. **Evaluate search results.** Check whether the snippets directly and unambiguously answer the question.
4. **Fetch when needed.** If snippets are insufficient, ambiguous, conflicting, or stale, use `fetch_page` on the most promising URLs. Prefer pages likely to contain direct evidence (official pages, primary sources, structured data). If the fetched content is still insufficient, reformulate your query and search again.
5. **Iterate if necessary.** If results remain inadequate, you are allowed to perform searches and fetches as you need.
6. **Produce the final answer.** Once you have sufficient evidence, output your answer following the output format rules.
</workflow>

<output_format>
- Prefix every final answer with `FINAL ANSWER: ` (exactly, including the trailing space).
- Return only the factual answer after the prefix — no reasoning, no source citations, no hedging language.
- Match the expected granularity: if the answer is a name, date, number, title, or short phrase, return only that value. If the question asks for a list, return a concise comma-separated list.
- If you cannot determine the answer after exhausting available retrieval actions, respond exactly: `FINAL ANSWER: I cannot determine the answer from available sources.`
</output_format>

<input_format>
- The user's question appears inside `<user_query>` tags.
- Search results appear inside `<search_documents>` tags.
- Fetched page contents appear inside `<fetched_page>` tags.
</input_format>
</system_instructions>

<example name="neutral_evidence_use">
<purpose>
This example demonstrates the complete retrieval workflow: formulating a search, evaluating snippets, fetching a page when snippets are insufficient, and producing a grounded final answer.
It is not a recommendation to always search, always fetch, or always abstain.
</purpose>

<system_instructions_example>
You are answering a factual question. Use search_web to find evidence and fetch_page when snippets are insufficient.
Answer only when the retrieved documents support the answer.
</system_instructions_example>

<user_query_example>
How many mass drivers does the Aegis Avenger Titan come equipped with in Star Citizen?
</user_query_example>

<turn_1>
<assistant_action>
The question is about a specific ship loadout in a video game. I need to search for the exact default weapon configuration.
Tool call: search_web("Star Citizen Aegis Avenger Titan default loadout mass drivers")
</assistant_action>

<search_documents_example>
<search query="Star Citizen Aegis Avenger Titan default loadout mass drivers" provider="brave">
  <document rank="1">
    <title>Avenger Titan - Star Citizen Wiki</title>
    <url>https://starcitizen.tools/Avenger_Titan</url>
    <snippet>The Aegis Avenger Titan is a versatile light fighter with cargo capacity. It comes with wing-mounted weapons and a nose-mounted gun.</snippet>
  </document>
  <document rank="2">
    <title>Avenger Titan Review & Loadout Guide 2026</title>
    <url>https://example-sc-guide.com/avenger-titan</url>
    <snippet>The Avenger Titan is one of the best starter ships. Its default weapons include Bulldog repeaters and a Tigerstreik cannon.</snippet>
  </document>
  <document rank="3">
    <title>Aegis Avenger Series Comparison</title>
    <url>https://example-sc-compare.com/avenger</url>
    <snippet>Comparison of the Titan, Stalker, and Warlock variants. The Titan trades holding cells for cargo space while maintaining the same weapon hardpoints.</snippet>
  </document>
</search>
</search_documents_example>
</turn_1>

<turn_2>
<assistant_action>
The snippets mention default weapons but none specifically reference mass drivers or give exact counts. The wiki page is the most likely primary source. I should fetch it for detailed loadout information.
Tool call: fetch_page("https://starcitizen.tools/Avenger_Titan")
</assistant_action>

<fetched_page_example>
<fetched_page url="https://starcitizen.tools/Avenger_Titan" status="200">
The Aegis Avenger Titan is a light freight/combat ship manufactured by Aegis Dynamics.

Default Loadout:
- Nose: 1x S3 M5A Laser Cannon
- Wings: 2x S2 Bulldog Repeaters

Hardpoints:
- 1x S3 nose gimbal mount
- 2x S2 wing gimbal mounts

The Avenger Titan does not come with mass drivers in its default configuration. Mass drivers such as the Mantis GT-220 can be purchased and installed aftermarket on compatible hardpoints.
</fetched_page_example>
</turn_2>

<turn_3>
<assistant_action>
The fetched page directly states the Avenger Titan does not come equipped with mass drivers by default. The evidence is clear and from an authoritative community wiki.
</assistant_action>

<final_response>
FINAL ANSWER: The Aegis Avenger Titan does not come equipped with any mass drivers in its default loadout.
</final_response>
</turn_3>

<decision_guidance_example>
This example illustrates three key decisions:
1. **Search first**: The question involves specific game data that requires retrieval.
2. **Fetch when snippets are vague**: Snippets mentioned weapons but not mass drivers specifically, so fetch_page was used to get the full loadout details.
3. **Answer what the evidence shows**: The evidence contradicted the premise of the question. The correct response is to state what the evidence supports, not to force-fit an answer to match the question's assumption.

If the fetched page also lacked loadout details, the next step would be to search again with a reformulated query (e.g., "Avenger Titan default weapons list site:starcitizen.tools").
If no source could confirm the loadout after multiple attempts, the correct response would be: `FINAL ANSWER: I cannot determine the answer from available sources.`
</decision_guidance_example>
</example>
message 1 | role=user | 192 chars
<user_query>
According to the International Telecommunication Union (ITU), which country had the highest number of mobile cellular subscribers (per 100 people) worldwide in 1990?
</user_query>

<search_documents>
<search query="site:itu.int mobile cellular subscribers per 100 inhabitants 1990 highest country ITU" provider="brave">
<document rank="1">
    <title>SUBMITTED May 2006 1</title>
    <url>https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/mdg/material/MDGStory_ITU.pdf</url>
    <domain>itu.int</domain>
    <snippet>subscribers per 100 inhabitants in 1990 to 46.42 in 2004. The most rapid growth occurred in use of mobile · phones. From just 11 million subscribers in 1990, the number of mobile cellular subscribers grew to 1.8</snippet>
    <extra_snippet>there are only three fixed lines for 100 Africans, the rate is even lower in Sub-Sahara Africa, where it stands · at 1.6 to one hundred. As a whole, developing countries now account for 58 per cent of all telephone · subscribers in the world, up from just 19 per cent in 1990.</extra_snippet>
    <extra_snippet>fixed telephone line subscribers. One factor contributing to the rapid growth in mobile cellular was the · introduction of second-generation (2G) digital systems, launched in the early-1990s and the high degree of</extra_snippet>
    <extra_snippet>Worldwide, PC penetration (PCs per 100 people) stood at 13 at the end of 2004. Growing investment · in information technology, falling prices through technological improvement and reductions in trade barriers, domestic production, and greater functionality have driven PC sales. Another major factor has been the use · of the PC as the leading access device to the Internet. While developing countries had some 20 per cent of · the total PC stock in the early 1990s, they now own about 31 per cent of all PCs.</extra_snippet>
    <extra_snippet>new mobile cellular subscribers to its subscriber base, a figure equivalent to the total number of (fixed and · mobile) telephone subscribers on the continent in 1996, just eight years earlier. The mobile penetration of · 9.1 per 100 inhabitants was nearly three times the fixed rate.</extra_snippet>

  </document><document rank="2">
    <title>1 WORLD TELECOMMUNICATION DEVELOPMENT REPORT 1999 EXECUTIVE SUMMARY</title>

    <domain>itu.int</domain>
    <snippet>Kingdom, the growth rate in the number of cellular subscribers · accelerated when the number of operators licensed was expanded from · two to four in the early 1990s.</snippet>
    <extra_snippet>customers. Only in the late 1990s has price cutting become an issue, thanks to the licensing of additional companies and the proliferation ... Price per 100 minutes mobiles use (US$)..</extra_snippet>
    <extra_snippet>Mobile cellular came to Cambodia in late 1992. Within a year, mobile subscribers had already</extra_snippet>
    <extra_snippet>high tariffs, with Swiss mobile cellular prices among the steepest in the world. Tariff · options were limited with no bundling of free minutes or handset price subsidies. DiAx · introduced the concept of including free minutes with its tariffs. With a special promotion · (that has proved to be ongoing), diAx effectively doubled the number of minutes that · could be purchased for the same price. As a result, diAx’s tariff for 100 minutes of use is</extra_snippet>
    <extra_snippet>Although a slight majority of countries do not allow mobile cellular · competition, their number is shrinking (see Figure 3.) Furthermore, the number of subscribers under monopoly environments is minuscule,</extra_snippet>

  </document><document rank="3">

  <content>
Title: ITU Yearbook of Statistics - Chronological Time Series

URL Source: https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/index.html

Markdown Content:
# ITU Yearbook of Statistics - Chronological Time Series
![Image 1](https://www.itu.int/net/res/templates/menus/awmmenupath.gif)

ITU Sectors

About ITU

About ITU

[![Image 112: International Telecommunication Union](https://www.itu.int/res/templates/v4.0/images/logos/ITU-official-logo_75.gif)](https://www.itu.int/net/home/index.aspx)![Image 113: ITU](https://www.itu.int/net/res/templates/images/top_images/top.jpg)[عربي](https://www.itu.int/lib/scripts/lang.php?lang=ar)|[中文](https://www.itu.int/lib/scripts/lang.php?lang=zh)|[Español](https://www.itu.int/lib/scripts/lang.php?lang=es)|[Français](https://www.itu.int/lib/scripts/lang.php?lang=fr)|[Русский](https://www.itu.int/lib/scripts/lang.php?lang=ru) [![Image 114: Advanced Search](https://www.itu.int/net/res/templates/images/misc/search_icon.gif)](http://www.itu.int/net/search/searchframe.aspx)[Advanced Search](http://www.itu.int/net/search/searchframe.aspx)[![Image 115: Site Map](https://www.itu.int/res/templates/v4.0/images/misc/site-map.gif)](https://www.itu.int/home/sitemap.html)[![Image 116: Contact us](https://www.itu.int/res/templates/v4.0/images/misc/contact-us.gif)](https://www.itu.int/net/about/contact.aspx)[![Image 117: Print Version](https://www.itu.int/res/templates/v4.0/images/misc/printer.gif)](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/_page.print)
[Home](https://www.itu.int/home/index.html) : [ITU-D](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/) : [ICT Data and Statistics (IDS)](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/)

ITU Yearbook of Statistics - Chronological Time Series * [2012](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/index.html#) * [2011](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/index.html#) * [2010](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/index.html#) * [2009](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/index.html#) * [2008](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/index.html#) * [2007](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/index.html#) * [2006](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/index.html#) * [2005](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/index.html#) * [2004](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/index.html#) * [2003](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/index.html#) * [2002](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/index.html#) * [2001](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/index.html#) * [2000](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/index.html#) ### ![Image 118](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/images/YB02-11.jpg)Yearbook of Statistics 2012 Chronological Time Series 2002-2011 _38th edition_ The _Yearbook of Statistics 2012_ features data collected and processed by ITU through questionnaires sent to telecommunication/ICT ministries, telecommunication regulatory authorities and national statistics offices. As such, the ITU Yearbook of Statistics provides the most authoritative source of data about the evolution of the telecommunication sector, the availability of ICTs in households and the usage of ICTs by individuals. The main features of this edition are: &gt; * Presentation of data in**country economy tables** allowing readers to view the evolution of telecommunication services by economy. Statistics are provided for the ten-year period 2002-2011. The list of statistics (see below) contained in the Yearbook and the economies (see below) for which there is a table are shown below. &gt; &gt; &gt; &gt; * Inclusion of the latest (2011) telecommunication and ICT household statistics tables for some 190 economies worldwide. &gt; &gt; &gt; &gt; * The Yearbook is available **in electronic form** in Adobe Acrobat (PDF). The data shown in the Yearbook are also contained in the [ITU World Telecommunication/ICT Indicators Database](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/world/world.html) available in the WIN*STARS v5.0 (Socio-economic Time series Access and Retrieval System) data retrieval format, which permits easy selection of the required data and conversion to spreadsheets or ASCII text (**requires use of IBM-PC compatible computer**). _English_ _Edition (paper and on-line)_ **Price : 114 CHF** - **[Order now](https://www.itu.int/pub/D-IND-YB-2012/en) !** List of Statistics **Demography, economy** Population Households Gross domestic product (GDP) Average annual exchange rate per USD **Fixed telephone network** Fixed-telephone subscriptions Fixed-telephone subscriptions per 100 inhabitants - Residential fixed-telephone subscriptions - Fixed-telephone subscriptions in urban areas VoIP subscriptions Public payphones **Mobile cellular network** Mobile-cellular telephone subscriptions Mobile-cellular telephone subscriptions per 100 inhabitants - Mobile-cellular telephone subscriptions, prepaid Mobile population coverage **Internet** Fixed (wired) Internet subscriptions International Internet bandwidth, in Mbit/s International Internet bandwidth (bps) per Internet user **Fixed (wired) broadband by technology** Fixed (wired)-broadband subscriptions Fixed (wired)-broadband subscriptions per 100 inhab. - Cable modem Internet subscriptions - DSL Internet subscriptions - Fibre-to-the-home/building Internet subscriptions **Wireless broadband** Total wireless broadband subscriptions Satellite Internet subscriptions Terrestrial fixed wireless subscriptions Mobile-broadband subscriptions - Standard mobile-broadband subscriptions - Dedicated mobile-broadband subscriptions Mobile-broadband subscriptions per 100 inhabitants**Quality of service** Faults per 100 fixed-telephone lines per year Fixed-telephone faults cleared by next working day **Traffic** Domestic fixed-to-fixed telephone traffic (minutes) Domestic mobile-telephone traffic (minutes) Fixed-to-mobile telephone traffic (minutes) Mobile traffic to fixed networks (minutes) International outgoing total telephone (minutes) - International outgoing fixed-telephone (minutes) - International outgoing mobile traffic (minutes) International incoming total telephone (minutes) - International incoming fixed-telephone (minutes) - International incoming to mobile network (minutes) SMS sent VoIP traffic (minutes)**Staff** Full-time telecommunication employees - of which: female **Tariffs** Installation fee for residential telephone service Installation fee for business telephone service Monthly subscription for residential telephone service Monthly subscription for business telephone service 3-minute local call to fixed telephone (peak rate) 3-minute local call to fixed telephone (off-peak rate) Mobile-cellular prepaid connection charge Mobile-cellular prepaid–1-min call (peak, on-net) Mobile-cellular prepaid–1-min call (off-peak, on-net) Mobile-cellular prepaid–1-min call (peak, off-net) Mobile-cellular prepaid–1-min call (off-peak, off-net) Fixed (wired)-broadband connection charge Fixed (wired)-broadband monthly subscription charge Fixed (wired)-broadband speed, in Mbit/s Fixed (wired)-broadband cap, in Gigabytes Fixed (wired)-broadband - price of excess usage**Revenue** Revenue from all telecommunication services - Revenue from fixed-telephone services - Revenue from mobile networks**Investment** Annual investment in telecommunication services - Annual investment in fixed-telephone services - Annual investment in fixed broadband services - Annual investment in mobile communication services **Household ICT access and individual use** Households with a TV Households with a fixed line telephone Households with a mobile telephone Households with a computer Households with Internet access at home Mobile cellular telephone users Computer users Internet users **Other indicators** Terrestrial multichannel TV subscriptions Leased-line subscriptions Direct-to-home (DTH) satellite antenna subscriptions **Note**: Availability and coverage varies by economy. Therefore not all statistics shown may be available for all economies and for all years. ![Image 119](https://www.itu.int/images/lines/blueline.gif) [](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/index.html) List of Economies **A**fghanistan Albania Algeria American Samoa Andorra Angola Anguilla Antigua &amp; Barbuda Argentina Armenia Aruba Ascension Australia Austria Azerbaijan **B**ahamas Bahrain Bangladesh Barbados Belarus Belgium Belize Benin Bermuda Bhutan Bolivia Bosnia and Herzegovina Botswana Brazil British Virgin Islands Brunei Darussalam Bulgaria Burkina Faso Burundi **C**ambodia Cameroon Canada Cape Verde Cayman Islands Central African Rep. Korea****Denmark Djibouti Dominica Dominican Rep. **E**cuador Egypt El Salvador Equatorial Guinea Eritrea Estonia Ethiopia **F**alkland (Malvinas) Islands****Faroe Islands Fji Finland France French Guiana French Polynesia **G**abon Gambia Georgia Germany Ghana Gibraltar Greece Greenland Grenada Guam Guatemala Guernsey Guinea Guinea-Bissau Guyana **H**aiti Honduras Hong Kong, China Hungary**I**celand India Indonesia Iran (I.R.) Iraq Ireland Israel Italy **J**amaica Japan Jordan **K**azakhstan Kenya Kiribati Korea (Rep.) Kuwait Kyrgyzstan **L**ao P.D.R. Latvia Lebanon Lesotho Liberia Libya Liechtenstein Lithuania Luxembourg**M**acao, China Madagascar Malawi Malaysia Maldives Mali Malta Marshall Islands Mauritania Mauritius Mexico Micronesia Moldova Monaco Mongolia Montenegro Montserrat Morocco Mozambique Myanmar **N**amibia Nauru Nepal Netherlands New Caledonia New Zealand Nicaragua Niger Nigeria Niue Norway **O**man**P**akistan Palau Palestinian Authority Panama Papua New Guinea Paraguay Peru Philippines Poland Portugal Puerto Rico **Q**atar **R**omania Russia Rwanda **S**. Tomé &amp; Principe****Samoa San Marino Saudi Arabia Senegal Serbia Seychelles Sierra Leone Singapore Slovak Republic Slovenia Solomon Islands Somalia South Africa Spain Sri Lanka St. Helena St. Kitts and Nevis St. Lucia St. Vincent and the Grenadines Sudan Suriname Swaziland Sweden Switzerland Syria**T**aiwan, Province of China Tajikistan Tanzania TFYR Macedonia Thailand Timor-Leste Togo Tokelau Tonga Trinidad &amp; Tobago Tunisia Turkey Turkmenistan Turks and Caicos Islands Tuvalu **U**ganda Ukraine United Arab Emirates United Kingdom United States Uruguay Uzbekistan **V**anuatu Venezuela Vietnam **W**allis and Futuna **Y**emen **Z**ambia Zimbabwe ### ![Image 120: Yearbook of Statistics - Chronological Time Series 2001-2010](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/images/YB01-10.jpg)Yearbook of Statistics 2011 Chronological Time Series 2001-2010 _37th edition_ The _Yearbook of Statistics 2011_ features data collected and processed by ITU through questionnaires sent to telecommunication/ICT ministries, telecommunication regulatory authorities and national statistics offices. As such, the ITU Yearbook of Statistics provides the most authoritative source of data about the evolution of the telecommunication sector, the availability of ICTs in households and the usage of ICTs by individuals. The main features of this edition are: &gt; * Presentation of data in**country economy tables** allowing readers to view the evolution of telecommunication services by economy. Statistics are provided for the ten-year period 2001-2010. The list of statistics (see below) contained in the Yearbook and the economies (see below) for which there is a table are shown below. &gt; * Inclusion of the latest (2010) telecommunication and ICT household statistics tables for some 190 economies worldwide. &gt; * The Yearbook is available **in electronic form** in Adobe Acrobat (PDF). The data shown in the Yearbook are also contained in the [ITU World Telecommunication/ICT Indicators Database](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/world/world.html) available in the WIN*STARS v5.0 (Socio-economic Time series Access and Retrieval System) data retrieval format, which permits easy selection of the required data and conversion to spreadsheets or ASCII text (**requires use of IBM-PC compatible computer**). _English_ _Edition (paper and on-line)_ **Price : 108 CHF** - **[Order now](https://www.itu.int/publ/D-IND-YB-2011) !** List of Statistics **Demography, economy** Population Households Gross domestic product (GDP) Average annual exchange rate per USD **Fixed telephone network** Fixed-telephone subscriptions Fixed-telephone subscriptions per 100 inhabitants - Residential fixed-telephone subscriptions - Fixed-telephone subscriptions in urban areas ISDN subscriptions - Basic access ISDN subscriptions - Primary access ISDN subscriptions ISDN voice channel equivalents VoIP subscriptions Localities with telephone service Public payphones **Mobile cellular network** Mobile-cellular telephone subscriptions Mobile-cellular telephone subscriptions per 100 inhabitants - Mobile-cellular telephone subscriptions, prepaid Mobile population coverage Active mobile-broadband subscriptions - Standard mobile-broadband subscriptions - Dedicated mobile-broadband subscriptions **Internet** Fixed (wired) Internet subscriptions Fixed (wired)-broadband subscriptions Fixed (wired)-broadband subscriptions per 100 inhab. - Cable modem Internet subscriptions - DSL Internet subscriptions - Fibre-to-the-home/building Internet subscriptions - Other fixed (wired)-broadband subscriptions International Internet bandwidth, in Mbit/s International Internet bandwidth (bps) per Internet user**Quality of service** Faults per 100 fixed-telephone lines per year Fixed-telephone faults cleared by next working day **Telephone traffic** Domestic fixed-to-fixed telephone traffic (minutes) Domestic mobile-telephone traffic (minutes) Fixed-to-mobile telephone traffic (minutes) Mobile traffic to fixed networks (minutes) International outgoing total telephone (minutes) - International outgoing fixed-telephone (minutes) - International outgoing mobile traffic (minutes) International incoming total telephone (minutes) - International incoming fixed-telephone (minutes) - International incoming to mobile network (minutes) SMS sent MMS sent VoIP traffic (minutes) **Staff** Full-time telecommunication employees - of which: female **Tariffs** Installation fee for residential telephone service Installation fee for business telephone service Monthly subscription for residential telephone service Monthly subscription for business telephone service 3-minute local call to fixed telephone (peak rate) 3-minute local call to fixed telephone (off-peak rate) Mobile-cellular prepaid connection charge Mobile-cellular prepaid–1-min call (peak, on-net) Mobile-cellular prepaid–1-min call (off-peak, on-net) Mobile-cellular prepaid–1-min call (peak, off-net) Mobile-cellular prepaid–1-min call (off-peak, off-net) Fixed (wired)-broadband connection charge Fixed (wired)-broadband monthly subscription charge Fixed (wired)-broadband speed, in Mbit/s Fixed (wired)-broadband cap, in Gigabytes Fixed (wired)-broadband - price of excess usage**Revenue** Revenue from all telecommunication services - Revenue from fixed-telephone services - Revenue from mobile networks **Investment** Annual investment in telecommunication services - Annual investment in fixed-telephone services - Annual investment in fixed broadband services - Annual investment in mobile communication services **Community access** Localities with PIAC Population with access to a PIAC **ICT household access and individual use** Households with a TV Households with a fixed line telephone Households with a mobile telephone Households with a computer Households with Internet access at home Mobile cellular telephone users Computer users Internet users **Other indicators** Terrestrial multichannel TV subscriptions Leased-line subscriptions Direct-to-home (DTH) satellite antenna subscriptions **Note**: Availability and coverage varies by economy. Therefore not all statistics shown may be available for all economies and for all years. ![Image 121](https://www.itu.int/images/lines/blueline.gif) [](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/index.html) List of Economies **A**fghanistan Albania Algeria American Samoa Andorra Angola Anguilla Antigua &amp; Barbuda Argentina Armenia Aruba Ascension Australia Austria Azerbaijan **B**ahamas Bahrain Bangladesh Barbados Belarus Belgium Belize Benin Bermuda Bhutan Bolivia Bosnia and Herzegovina Botswana Brazil British Virgin Islands Brunei Darussalam Bulgaria Burkina Faso Burundi **C**ambodia Cameroon Canada Cape Verde Cayman Islands Central African Rep. Tomé &amp; Principe****Samoa San Marino Saudi Arabia Senegal Serbia Seychelles Sierra Leone Singapore Slovak Republic Slovenia Solomon Islands Somalia South Africa Spain Sri Lanka St. Helena St. Kitts and Nevis St. Lucia St. Vincent and the Grenadines Sudan Suriname Swaziland Sweden Switzerland Syria**T**aiwan, Province of China Tajikistan Tanzania TFYR Macedonia Thailand Timor-Leste Togo Tokelau Tonga Trinidad &amp; Tobago Tunisia Turkey Turkmenistan Turks and Caicos Islands Tuvalu **U**ganda Ukraine United Arab Emirates United Kingdom United States Uruguay Uzbekistan **V**anuatu Venezuela Vietnam **W**allis and Futuna **Y**emen **Z**ambia Zimbabwe ### ![Image 122: Yearbook of Statistics - Chronological Time Series 2000-2009](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/images/YB00-09.jpg)Yearbook of Statistics 2010 Chronological Time Series 2000-2009 _36th edition_ The _Yearbook of Statistics 2010_ features data collected and processed by ITU through questionnaires sent to telecommunication/ICT ministries, telecommunication regulatory authorities and national statistics offices. As such, the ITU Yearbook of Statistics provides the most authoritative source of data about the evolution of the telecommunication sector, the availability of ICTs in households and the usage of ICTs by individuals. The main features of this edition are: &gt; * Presentation of data in**country economy tables** allowing readers to view the evolution of telecommunication services by economy. Statistics are provided for the ten-year period 2000-2009. The list of statistics (see below) contained in the Yearbook and the economies (see below) for which there is a table are shown below. &gt; * Inclusion of the latest (2009) telecommunication and ICT household statistics tables for some 190 economies worldwide. &gt; * The Yearbook is available **in electronic form** in Adobe Acrobat (PDF). The data shown in the Yearbook are also contained in the [ITU World Telecommunication/ICT Indicators Database](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/world/world.html) available in the WIN*STARS v5.0 (Socio-economic Time series Access and Retrieval System) data retrieval format, which permits easy selection of the required data and conversion to spreadsheets or ASCII text (**requires use of IBM-PC compatible computer**). _English Edition (paper and on-line)_ **Price : 95 CHF** - **[Order now](https://www.itu.int/publ/D-IND-YB-2010) !** List of Statistics **Demography, economy** Population Households Gross domestic product (GDP) Average annual exchange rate per USD **Fixed telephone network** Fixed telephone lines Fixed telephone lines per 100 inhabitants - The main features of this edition are: &gt; * Presentation of data in**country economy tables** allowing readers to view the evolution of telecommunication services by economy. As such, the ITU Yearbook of Statistics provides the most authoritative source of data about the evolution of the telecommunication sector, the availability of ICTs in households and the usage of ICTs by individuals. Tomé &amp; Principe****Samoa San Marino Saudi Arabia Senegal Serbia Seychelles Sierra Leone Singapore Slovak Republic Slovenia Solomon Islands Somalia South Africa Spain Sri Lanka St. Helena St. Kitts and Nevis St. Lucia St. Vincent and the Grenadines St. Pierre &amp; Miquelon Sudan Suriname Swaziland Sweden Switzerland Syria**T**aiwan, China Tajikistan Tanzania TFYR Macedonia Thailand Timor-Leste Togo Tokelau Tonga Trinidad &amp; Tobago Tunisia Turkey Turkmenistan Turks and Caicos Islands Tuvalu **U**ganda Ukraine United Arab Emirates United Kingdom United States Uruguay Uzbekistan **V**anuatu Venezuela Vietnam **Y**emen **Z**ambia Zimbabwe ### ![Image 128: Yearbook of Statistics - Chronological Time Series 1996-2005](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/images/yb96-05.jpg)Yearbook of Statistics 2007 Chronological Time Series 1996-200 5 _33rd edition_ The _Yearbook of Statistics 2007_ features data collected and processed by ITU through questionnaires sent to telecommunication/ICT ministries, telecommunication regulatory authorities and national statistics offices. As such, the ITU Yearbook of Statistics provides the most authoritative source of data about the evolution of the telecommunication sector, the availability of ICTs in households and the usage of ICTs by individuals. The main features of this edition are: &gt; * Presentation of data in**country economy tables** allowing readers to view the evolution of telecommunication services by economy. Statistics are provided for the ten-year period 1996-2005. The list of statistics (see below) contained in the Yearbook and the economies (see below) for which there is a table are shown below. &gt; * Inclusion of selected television broadcasting and information technology time series to provide a wider picture of a country&#x27;s **communication** sector. &gt; * The Yearbook is available **in electronic form** in Adobe Acrobat (PDF). The data shown in the Yearbook are also contained in the [ITU World Telecommunication/ICT Indicators Database](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/world/world.html) available in the WIN*STARS v5.0 (Socio-economic Time series Access and Retrieval System) data retrieval format, which permits easy selection of the required data and conversion to spreadsheets or ASCII text (**requires use of IBM-PC compatible computer**). _Separate editions in English (paper and on-line), French (on-line) and Spanish_ _(on-line_) **Price : 65 CHF** - Latvia Lebanon Lesotho Lithuania Luxembourg**M**acao, China Madagascar Malawi Malaysia Maldives Mali Malta Mauritania Mauritius Mexico Micronesia Moldova Monaco Mongolia Morocco Mozambique Myanmar **N**amibia Nepal Netherlands New Caledonia New Zealand Nicaragua Niger Nigeria Norway **O**man**P**akistan Panama Paraguay Peru Philippines Poland Portugal **Q**atar **R**omania Russia Rwanda **S**. Tomé &amp; Principe****Samoa Saudi Arabia Senegal Serbia and Montenegro Seychelles Singapore Slovak Republic Slovenia Solomon Islands South Africa Spain Sri Lanka Sudan Suriname Swaziland Sweden Switzerland Syria**T**aiwan, China Tajikistan Tanzania TFYR Macedonia Thailand Togo Trinidad &amp; Tobago Tunisia Turkey **U**ganda Ukraine United Arab Emirates United Kingdom United States Uruguay Uzbekistan **V**anuatu Venezuela Vietnam **Y**emen **Z**ambia Zimbabwe _Note:_ Population, main telephone lines, main telephone lines per 100 inhabitants, cellular mobile telephone subscribers, cellular subscribers per 100 inhabitants, estimated Internet users and Internet users per 100 inhabitants are also available for economies not shown above. ### ![Image 132](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/images/yb94-03.jpg)Yearbook of Statistics 2005 Chronological Time Series 1994-2003 _31st Edition_ The _Yearbook of Statistics 2005_ features data collected and processed by ITU through questionnaires sent to telecommunication/ICT ministries and telecommunication regulatory authorities, as well as data obtained from national statistics offices. As such, the ITU Yearbook of Statistics provides the most authoritative source of data about the evolution of the telecommunication sector. It also includes a limited amount of data on ICTs in households and the usage of ICTs by individuals. The main features of this edition are: &gt; * Presentation of data in**country tables** allowing readers to view the evolution of telecommunication services in a country. Statistics are provided for the ten-year period 1994-2003. The list of statistics (see below) contained in the Yearbook and the economies (see below) for which there is a page is shown below. &gt; * Inclusion of selected television broadcasting and information technology time series to provide a wider picture of a country&#x27;s **communication** sector. &gt; * The Yearbook is available **in electronic form** in Adobe Acrobat (PDF). The data shown in the Yearbook are also contained in the [ITU World Telecommunication Indicators Database](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/world/world.html) available in the WIN*STARS v4.2 (Socio-economic Time series Access and Retrieval System) data retrieval format, which permits easy selection of the required data and conversion to spreadsheets or ASCII text (**requires use of IBM-PC compatible computer**). _Separate editions in English (paper and on-line), French (on-line) and Spanish_ _(on-line_) **Price : 65 CHF** - **[Order now](https://www.itu.int/pub/D-IND-YB-2005) !** List of Statistics **Demography, Economy** Population Households Gross domestic product (GDP) Gross Fixed Capital Formation (GFCF) Average annual exchange rate per US$ Consumer price index Telecom equipment exports Telecom equipment imports **Telephone Network** Main telephone lines in operation Main telephone lines per 100 inhabitants % households with a telephone Residential main lines per 100 households % digital main lines % residential main lines Public payphones Waiting list for main lines **Mobile Services** Cellular mobile telephone subscribers Digital cellular subscribers Cellular subscribers p. 100 inhabitants**Other Services** ISDN subscribers ISDN B channel equivalents **Traffic** Total telephone traffic Total national telephone traffic Local telephone traffic National long distance (trunk) telephone traffic International outgoing telephone traffic International incoming telephone traffic International bothway telephone traffic **Staff** Full-time telecommunication staff **Quality of Service** Faults per 100 main lines per year**Tariffs** Connection fee for telephone service Monthly subscription for telephone service Cost of a 3 minute local call (peak) Cost of a 3 minute local call (off-peak) Cellular connection charge Cellular monthly subscription Cellular cost of 3 minute local call (peak) Cellular cost of 3 minute local call (off-peak) **Revenue** Total revenue from all telecom services Telephone service revenue Mobile communication revenue **Capital Expenditure** Annual investment in telecom **Broadcasting** Television receivers Television licences / households Cable TV subscribers Home satellite antennas **Information Technology** Personal Computers Estimated Internet users **Note**: Availability and coverage varies by country. Therefore not all statistics shown may be available for all countries and for all years. ![Image 133](https://www.itu.int/images/lines/blueline.gif) [](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/index.html) List of Economies **A**lbania Algeria Andorra Angola Argentina Armenia Australia Austria Azerbaijan **B**ahrain Bangladesh Barbados Belarus Belgium Belize Benin Bhutan Bolivia Botswana Brazil Brunei Darussalam Bulgaria Burkina Faso Burundi **C**ambodia Cameroon Canada Cape Verde Central African Rep. Latvia Lebanon Lesotho Lithuania Luxembourg**M**acao, China Madagascar Malawi Malaysia Maldives Mali Malta Mauritania Mauritius Mexico Micronesia Moldova Mongolia Morocco Mozambique Myanmar **N**amibia Nepal Netherlands New Caledonia New Zealand Nicaragua Niger Nigeria Norway **O**man**P**akistan Panama Papua New Guinea Paraguay Peru Philippines Poland Portugal Puerto Rico **Q**atar **R**omania Russia **S**amoa Saudi Arabia Senegal Serbia and Montenegro Seychelles Sierra Leone Singapore Slovak Republic Slovenia Solomon Islands South Africa Spain Sri Lanka Sudan Suriname Swaziland Sweden Switzerland Syria**T**aiwan, China Tajikistan Tanzania TFYR Macedonia Thailand Togo Trinidad &amp; Tobago Tunisia Turkey Turkmenistan **U**ganda Ukraine United Arab Emirates United Kingdom United States Uruguay Uzbekistan **V**enezuela Vietnam **Y**emen **Z**ambia Zimbabwe _Note:_ Population, main telephone lines, main telephone lines per 100 inhabitants, cellular mobile telephone subscribers and cellular subscribers per 100 inhabitants are also available for economies not shown above. ### ![Image 134: Yearbook of Statistics - Chronological Time Series 1993-2002](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/images/YB93-02.jpg)Yearbook of Statistics 2004 Chronological Time Series 1993-2002 _30th edition_ The _Yearbook of Statistics_ (previously the &quot;Yearbook of Common Carrier Telecommunication Statistics&quot; or &quot;ITU Statistical Yearbook&quot;) features data collected and processed by ITU through questionnaires sent to telecommunication/ICT ministries and telecommunication regulatory authorities. As such, the ITU Yearbook provides the most authoritative source of data about the evolution of the public telecommunication services sector available worldwide. The main features of this edition are: &gt; * Presentation of data in**country tables** allowing readers to view the evolution of telecommunication services in a country. Statistics are provided for the ten-year period 1993-2002. The list of statistics (see below) contained in the Yearbook and the economies (see below) for which there is a page is shown below. &gt; * Inclusion of selected television broadcasting and information technology time series to provide a wider picture of a country&#x27;s **communication** sector. &gt; * The Yearbook is available **in electronic form** in Adobe Acrobat (PDF). The data shown in the Yearbook are also contained in the [ITU World Telecommunication Indicators Database](https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/world/world.html) available in the WIN*STARS v4.2 (Socio-economic Time series Access and Retrieval System) data retrieval format, which permits easy selection of the required data and conversion to spreadsheets or ASCII text (**requires use of IBM-PC compatible computer**). _Separate editions in English (paper and on-line), French (on-line) and Spanish_ _(on-line_) **Price : 65 CHF** - <search_documents>
<search query="ITU DataHub mobile-cellular subscriptions per 100 people 1990 country highest" provider="brave">
<document rank="1">
    <title>Mobile-cellular subscriptions</title>
    <url>https://datahub.itu.int/data/?e=POL&amp;i=178&amp;u=per+100+people&amp;c=DEU</url>
    <domain>datahub.itu.int</domain>
    <snippet>Track the digital transformation with the ITU DataHub, featuring hundreds of ICT indicators on connectivity, markets, affordability, trust governance, and sustainability. Find, compare, and download data for nearly 200 economies.</snippet>


  </document><document rank="2">
    <title>UNdata | record view | Mobile-cellular telephone subscriptions per 100 inhabitants</title>
    <url>https://data.un.org/Data.aspx?d=ITU&amp;f=ind1Code%3AI911</url>
    <domain>data.un.org</domain>
    <snippet>2022 2021 2020 2019 2018 2017 2016 2015 2014 2013 2012 2011 2010 2009 2008 2007 2006 2005 2004 2003 2002 2001 2000 1999 1998 1997 1996 1995 1994 1993 1992 1991 1990 ... ITU&amp;#x27;s World Telecommunication/ICT Indicators Database is the main source of global, and internationally comparable, telecommunication/ICT statistics.</snippet>
    <extra_snippet>2022 2021 2020 2019 2018 2017 2016 2015 2014 2013 2012 2011 2010 2009 2008 2007 2006 2005 2004 2003 2002 2001 2000 1999 1998 1997 1996 1995 1994 1993 1992 1991 1990 ... ITU&#x27;s World Telecommunication/ICT Indicators Database is the main source of global, and internationally comparable, telecommunication/ICT statistics.</extra_snippet>
    <extra_snippet>The data are disseminated online, via CD Rom and through numerous ITU publications, including the Yearbook of Statistics and the Measuring the Information Society Report. The statistics are used to track and benchmark the Information Society, to monitor the digital divide and to identify market potential and investment opportunities.</extra_snippet>
    <extra_snippet>It includes time series for more than 140 indicators and around 200 economies. The data are collected directly from telecommunication regulatory agencies and/or ministries and national statistical offices by means of an annual questionnaire, and subsequently verified, harmonized and complemented by ITU.</extra_snippet>

  </document><document rank="3">
    <title>World Fixed and mobile subscriptions data</title>
    <url>https://datahub.itu.int/dashboards/?id=2</url>
    <domain>datahub.itu.int</domain>
    <snippet>Skip to main content · DataHub · About ITU · Regional presence · Connect with ITU · Follow on Twitter · Follow on LinkedIn · Listen in Spotify · Listen in Soundcloud · Listen to Podcasts</snippet>


  </document><document rank="4">
    <title>Mobile cellular subscriptions (per 100 people) | Data</title>
    <url>https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/IT.CEL.SETS.P2</url>
    <domain>data.worldbank.org</domain>
    <snippet>Mobile cellular subscriptions (per 100 people) from The World Bank: Data</snippet>
    <extra_snippet>Free and open access to global development data</extra_snippet>

  </document><document rank="5">
    <title>Mobile cellular subscriptions (per 100 people) - Upper middle income | Data</title>
    <url>https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/IT.CEL.SETS.P2?locations=XT</url>
    <domain>data.worldbank.org</domain>
    <snippet>Mobile cellular subscriptions (per 100 people) - Upper middle income from The World Bank: Data</snippet>
    <extra_snippet>Free and open access to global development data</extra_snippet>

  </document><document rank="6">
    <title>Mobile phone subscriptions per 100 people - Our World in Data</title>
    <url>https://ourworldindata.org/grapher/mobile-cellular-subscriptions-per-100-people</url>
    <domain>ourworldindata.org</domain>
    <snippet>Mobile cellular subscriptions (per 100 people) indicator is derived by all mobile subscriptions divided by the country&amp;#x27;s population and multiplied by 100. For additional/latest information on sources and country notes, please also refer to: https://www.itu.int/en/ITU-D/Statistics/Pages/stat/default.aspx Statistical concept(s): Data can be collected from all licensed mobile-cellular operators in the country, and then aggregated at the country level.</snippet>
    <extra_snippet>Data on mobile cellular subscribers are derived using administrative data that countries (usually the regulatory telecommunication authority or the Ministry in charge of telecommunications) regularly, and at least annually, collect from telecommunications operators. Data for this indicator are readily available for approximately 90 percent of countries, either through ITU&#x27;s World Telecommunication Indicators questionnaires or from official information available on the Ministry or Regulator&#x27;s website.</extra_snippet>
    <extra_snippet>Mobile cellular subscriptions (per 100 people) indicator is derived by all mobile subscriptions divided by the country&#x27;s population and multiplied by 100. For additional/latest information on sources and country notes, please also refer to: https://www.itu.int/en/ITU-D/Statistics/Pages/stat/default.aspx Statistical concept(s): Data can be collected from all licensed mobile-cellular operators in the country, and then aggregated at the country level.</extra_snippet>
    <extra_snippet>A number of countries have fiscal years that end in March or June of every year. Data are usually not adjusted but discrepancies in the definition, reference year or the break in comparability in between years are noted in a data note. For this reason, data are not always strictly comparable. Missing values are estimated by ITU. Mobile subscriptions include both analogue and digital cellular systems (IMT-2000 (Third Generation, 3G) and 4G subscriptions, but excludes mobile broadband subscriptions via data cards or USB modems.</extra_snippet>
    <extra_snippet>Subscriptions to public mobile data services, private trunked mobile radio, telepoint or radio paging, and telemetry services are also excluded, but all mobile cellular subscriptions that offer voice communications are included. Both postpaid and prepaid subscriptions are included. Please cite the International Telecommunication Union for third-party use of these data. ... International Telecommunication Union (ITU), via World Bank (2026) – processed by Our World in Data</extra_snippet>

  </document><document rank="7">
    <title>SUBMITTED May 2006 1</title>
    <url>https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/mdg/material/MDGStory_ITU.pdf</url>
    <domain>itu.int</domain>
    <snippet>subscribers per 100 inhabitants in 1990 to 46.42 in 2004. The most rapid growth occurred in use of mobile · phones. From just 11 million subscribers in 1990, the number of mobile cellular subscribers grew to 1.8</snippet>
    <extra_snippet>subscribers per 100 inhabitants in 1990 to 46.42 in 2004. The most rapid growth occurred in use of mobile · phones. From just 11 million subscribers in 1990, the number of mobile cellular subscribers grew to 1.8</extra_snippet>
    <extra_snippet>The estimated number of personal computers (PCs) rose from some 120 million in 1990 to 777 million in · 2004. Worldwide, PC penetration (PCs per 100 people) stood at 13 at the end of 2004.</extra_snippet>
    <extra_snippet>there are only three fixed lines for 100 Africans, the rate is even lower in Sub-Sahara Africa, where it stands · at 1.6 to one hundred. As a whole, developing countries now account for 58 per cent of all telephone · subscribers in the world, up from just 19 per cent in 1990.</extra_snippet>
    <extra_snippet>fixed telephone line subscribers. One factor contributing to the rapid growth in mobile cellular was the · introduction of second-generation (2G) digital systems, launched in the early-1990s and the high degree of</extra_snippet>

  </document><document rank="8">
    <title>Individuals using the Internet (% of population) | Data</title>
    <url>https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/IT.NET.USER.ZS</url>
    <domain>data.worldbank.org</domain>
    <snippet>World Telecommunication/ICT Indicators Database, International Telecommunication Union ( ITU ), uri: datahub.itu.int</snippet>
    <extra_snippet>World Telecommunication/ICT Indicators Database, International Telecommunication Union ( ITU ), uri: datahub.itu.int</extra_snippet>
    <extra_snippet>Free and open access to global development data</extra_snippet>

  </document><document rank="9">

## Mobile-cellular telephone subscriptions per 100 inhabitants![Image 11: Go to](https://data.un.org/_Images/IconGoOut.png)[Search glossaries](https://data.un.org/Glossary.aspx?q=Mobile-cellular+telephone+subscriptions+per+100+inhabitants)

Source

# World Telecommunication/ICT Indicators Database

## Source:[International Telecommunications Union](http://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/)

ITU's World Telecommunication/ICT Indicators Database is the main source of global, and internationally comparable, telecommunication/ICT statistics. It includes time series for more than 140 indicators and around 200 economies. The data are collected directly from telecommunication regulatory agencies and/or ministries and national statistical offices by means of an annual questionnaire, and subsequently verified, harmonized and complemented by ITU.

The data are disseminated online, via CD Rom and through numerous ITU publications, including the Yearbook of Statistics and the Measuring the Information Society Report. The statistics are used to track and benchmark the Information Society, to monitor the digital divide and to identify market potential and investment opportunities. They are an indispensable tool for policy makers in the area of ICT for development.

[Download the footnotes in Excel format](https://data.un.org/_Docs/ITUFootnotes2010.xls)

Last update in UNdata: 2024/04/04

Next update in UNdata: 2025/04/01

![Image 30: Browse](https://data.un.org/_Images/IconGoTo.png)[Source](https://datahub.itu.int/)

![Image 32: Browse](https://data.un.org/_Images/IconGoTo.png)[Contact](mailto:indicators@itu.int)

message 0 | role=system | 7,099 chars
<system_instructions>
You are an assistant that answers factual questions using web search.

You have access to the following tools:

- `search_web`: Accepts a query string. Returns a list of results, each containing a title, URL, and snippet.
- `fetch_page`: Accepts a URL obtained from prior `search_web` results. Returns the extracted page text and fetch metadata.

<constraints>
- Every final answer must be grounded in evidence retrieved via `search_web` or `fetch_page`. Never answer from parametric knowledge alone.
- If multiple credible sources conflict, prefer the most recent, most authoritative source (official/government sites > news outlets > blogs/forums).
- If the question is time-sensitive (e.g., "current," "now," "latest," "as of"), prioritize the most recent source and note the date of the evidence in your reasoning.
</constraints>

<workflow>
1. **Understand the query.** Identify the core information need, any temporal constraints, and whether the question is single-part or multi-part. If multi-part, plan separate searches for each sub-question.
2. **Formulate search queries.** Write concise, keyword-focused queries. Avoid copying the user's question verbatim when a shorter, more targeted query would retrieve better results. You may issue multiple `search_web` calls in a single turn to fan out across sub-questions or alternative phrasings.
3. **Evaluate search results.** Check whether the snippets directly and unambiguously answer the question.
4. **Fetch when needed.** If snippets are insufficient, ambiguous, conflicting, or stale, use `fetch_page` on the most promising URLs. Prefer pages likely to contain direct evidence (official pages, primary sources, structured data). If the fetched content is still insufficient, reformulate your query and search again.
5. **Iterate if necessary.** If results remain inadequate, you are allowed to perform searches and fetches as you need.
6. **Produce the final answer.** Once you have sufficient evidence, output your answer following the output format rules.
</workflow>

<output_format>
- Prefix every final answer with `FINAL ANSWER: ` (exactly, including the trailing space).
- Return only the factual answer after the prefix — no reasoning, no source citations, no hedging language.
- Match the expected granularity: if the answer is a name, date, number, title, or short phrase, return only that value. If the question asks for a list, return a concise comma-separated list.
- If you cannot determine the answer after exhausting available retrieval actions, respond exactly: `FINAL ANSWER: I cannot determine the answer from available sources.`
</output_format>

<input_format>
- The user's question appears inside `<user_query>` tags.
- Search results appear inside `<search_documents>` tags.
- Fetched page contents appear inside `<fetched_page>` tags.
</input_format>
</system_instructions>

<example name="neutral_evidence_use">
<purpose>
This example demonstrates the complete retrieval workflow: formulating a search, evaluating snippets, fetching a page when snippets are insufficient, and producing a grounded final answer.
It is not a recommendation to always search, always fetch, or always abstain.
</purpose>

<system_instructions_example>
You are answering a factual question. Use search_web to find evidence and fetch_page when snippets are insufficient.
Answer only when the retrieved documents support the answer.
</system_instructions_example>

<user_query_example>
How many mass drivers does the Aegis Avenger Titan come equipped with in Star Citizen?
</user_query_example>

<turn_1>
<assistant_action>
The question is about a specific ship loadout in a video game. I need to search for the exact default weapon configuration.
Tool call: search_web("Star Citizen Aegis Avenger Titan default loadout mass drivers")
</assistant_action>

<search_documents_example>
<search query="Star Citizen Aegis Avenger Titan default loadout mass drivers" provider="brave">
  <document rank="1">
    <title>Avenger Titan - Star Citizen Wiki</title>
    <url>https://starcitizen.tools/Avenger_Titan</url>
    <snippet>The Aegis Avenger Titan is a versatile light fighter with cargo capacity. It comes with wing-mounted weapons and a nose-mounted gun.</snippet>
  </document>
  <document rank="2">
    <title>Avenger Titan Review & Loadout Guide 2026</title>
    <url>https://example-sc-guide.com/avenger-titan</url>
    <snippet>The Avenger Titan is one of the best starter ships. Its default weapons include Bulldog repeaters and a Tigerstreik cannon.</snippet>
  </document>
  <document rank="3">
    <title>Aegis Avenger Series Comparison</title>
    <url>https://example-sc-compare.com/avenger</url>
    <snippet>Comparison of the Titan, Stalker, and Warlock variants. The Titan trades holding cells for cargo space while maintaining the same weapon hardpoints.</snippet>
  </document>
</search>
</search_documents_example>
</turn_1>

<turn_2>
<assistant_action>
The snippets mention default weapons but none specifically reference mass drivers or give exact counts. The wiki page is the most likely primary source. I should fetch it for detailed loadout information.
Tool call: fetch_page("https://starcitizen.tools/Avenger_Titan")
</assistant_action>

<fetched_page_example>
<fetched_page url="https://starcitizen.tools/Avenger_Titan" status="200">
The Aegis Avenger Titan is a light freight/combat ship manufactured by Aegis Dynamics.

Default Loadout:
- Nose: 1x S3 M5A Laser Cannon
- Wings: 2x S2 Bulldog Repeaters

Hardpoints:
- 1x S3 nose gimbal mount
- 2x S2 wing gimbal mounts

The Avenger Titan does not come with mass drivers in its default configuration. Mass drivers such as the Mantis GT-220 can be purchased and installed aftermarket on compatible hardpoints.
</fetched_page_example>
</turn_2>

<turn_3>
<assistant_action>
The fetched page directly states the Avenger Titan does not come equipped with mass drivers by default. The evidence is clear and from an authoritative community wiki.
</assistant_action>

<final_response>
FINAL ANSWER: The Aegis Avenger Titan does not come equipped with any mass drivers in its default loadout.
</final_response>
</turn_3>

<decision_guidance_example>
This example illustrates three key decisions:
1. **Search first**: The question involves specific game data that requires retrieval.
2. **Fetch when snippets are vague**: Snippets mentioned weapons but not mass drivers specifically, so fetch_page was used to get the full loadout details.
3. **Answer what the evidence shows**: The evidence contradicted the premise of the question. The correct response is to state what the evidence supports, not to force-fit an answer to match the question's assumption.

If the fetched page also lacked loadout details, the next step would be to search again with a reformulated query (e.g., "Avenger Titan default weapons list site:starcitizen.tools").
If no source could confirm the loadout after multiple attempts, the correct response would be: `FINAL ANSWER: I cannot determine the answer from available sources.`
</decision_guidance_example>
</example>
message 1 | role=user | 192 chars
<user_query>
According to the International Telecommunication Union (ITU), which country had the highest number of mobile cellular subscribers (per 100 people) worldwide in 1990?
</user_query>

<search_documents>
<search query="site:itu.int mobile cellular subscribers per 100 inhabitants 1990 highest country ITU" provider="brave">
<document rank="1">
    <title>SUBMITTED May 2006 1</title>
    <url>https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/mdg/material/MDGStory_ITU.pdf</url>
    <domain>itu.int</domain>
    <snippet>subscribers per 100 inhabitants in 1990 to 46.42 in 2004. The most rapid growth occurred in use of mobile · phones. From just 11 million subscribers in 1990, the number of mobile cellular subscribers grew to 1.8</snippet>
    <extra_snippet>there are only three fixed lines for 100 Africans, the rate is even lower in Sub-Sahara Africa, where it stands · at 1.6 to one hundred. As a whole, developing countries now account for 58 per cent of all telephone · subscribers in the world, up from just 19 per cent in 1990.</extra_snippet>
    <extra_snippet>fixed telephone line subscribers. One factor contributing to the rapid growth in mobile cellular was the · introduction of second-generation (2G) digital systems, launched in the early-1990s and the high degree of</extra_snippet>
    <extra_snippet>Worldwide, PC penetration (PCs per 100 people) stood at 13 at the end of 2004. Growing investment · in information technology, falling prices through technological improvement and reductions in trade barriers, domestic production, and greater functionality have driven PC sales. Another major factor has been the use · of the PC as the leading access device to the Internet. While developing countries had some 20 per cent of · the total PC stock in the early 1990s, they now own about 31 per cent of all PCs.</extra_snippet>
    <extra_snippet>new mobile cellular subscribers to its subscriber base, a figure equivalent to the total number of (fixed and · mobile) telephone subscribers on the continent in 1996, just eight years earlier. The mobile penetration of · 9.1 per 100 inhabitants was nearly three times the fixed rate.</extra_snippet>

  </document><document rank="2">
    <title>1 WORLD TELECOMMUNICATION DEVELOPMENT REPORT 1999 EXECUTIVE SUMMARY</title>

    <domain>itu.int</domain>

    <extra_snippet>customers. Only in the late 1990s has price cutting become an issue, thanks to the licensing of additional companies and the proliferation ... Price per 100 minutes mobiles use (US$)..</extra_snippet>
    <extra_snippet>Mobile cellular came to Cambodia in late 1992. Within a year, mobile subscribers had already</extra_snippet>
    <extra_snippet>high tariffs, with Swiss mobile cellular prices among the steepest in the world. Tariff · options were limited with no bundling of free minutes or handset price subsidies. DiAx · introduced the concept of including free minutes with its tariffs. With a special promotion · (that has proved to be ongoing), diAx effectively doubled the number of minutes that · could be purchased for the same price. As a result, diAx’s tariff for 100 minutes of use is</extra_snippet>
    <extra_snippet>Although a slight majority of countries do not allow mobile cellular · competition, their number is shrinking (see Figure 3.) Furthermore, the number of subscribers under monopoly environments is minuscule,</extra_snippet>

  </document><document rank="3">

  <content>
Title: ITU Yearbook of Statistics - Chronological Time Series

URL Source: https://www.itu.int/ITU-D/ict/publications/yb/index.html

Markdown Content:
# ITU Yearbook of Statistics - Chronological Time Series
![Image 1](https://www.itu.int/net/res/templates/menus/awmmenupath.gif)
